title: Visible Abstention Selector Benchmark
question: Can a selector improve precision without hiding failures behind an unreported commit-rate drop?
hypothesis: Explicit abstention labels and coverage reporting will separate useful evidence selection from silent cherry-picking.
success_signal: A visible-only selector beats first-visible, shortest-visible, and random-order baselines at matched coverage.
failure_signal: Accuracy gains disappear when coverage is matched or family-held-out evaluation is applied.
next_step: Run idea intake against selector and oracle-ceiling anchor experiments.

title: Expected-Output-Free Counterexample Tests
question: Can acquired tests distinguish candidates without requiring hidden expected outputs?
hypothesis: Agreement, invariants, metamorphic checks, and execution consistency can improve selection while preserving deployability.
success_signal: Selection improves over random acquisition and fixed-order acquisition under the same evidence budget.
failure_signal: The method helps only when hidden answers or task-specific leaks enter the evidence loop.
next_step: Start from active counterexample and model-in-loop counterexample experiments.

title: Independent Consensus Versus Selector Pool
question: When does independent implementation consensus beat a trained selector on the same candidate pool?
hypothesis: Consensus helps when failures are uncorrelated, while learned selectors help when visible evidence is structured but sparse.
success_signal: The comparison identifies regimes where consensus, selection, or hybrid routing dominates.
failure_signal: Consensus gains vanish after controlling for extra samples or shared prompt artifacts.
next_step: Use existing independent retrieval and counterexample selection anchors as prior evidence.

title: Deployable Gap Scorecards
question: Which oracle-ceiling results remain promising after visible-only constraints are made explicit?
hypothesis: A standard scorecard will reveal reusable mechanisms and retire variants that depend on non-deployable evidence.
success_signal: Every reviewed ceiling result has a clear keep, rerun, or retire decision.
failure_signal: Scorecards cannot distinguish mechanism promise from hidden-label dependence.
next_step: Seed from selector, code ABI, and coverage-gate reports.

title: Typed Versus Latent Versus Text Compiler Suite
question: Which structured representation causes generalization: typed bytecode, latent slots, text programs, or execution feedback?
hypothesis: Typed state helps most under compositional shifts, while latent state helps only when supervision and task families align.
success_signal: At least one representation has a reproducible advantage across non-IID splits and matched budget.
failure_signal: All gains collapse under shared splits or shuffled/corrupted state controls.
next_step: Use compiler, latent executor, and structured slot anchors to define the suite.

title: Compiler Shift Replication Grid
question: Which compiler-style gains survive seed, length, operator, and paraphrase shifts?
hypothesis: Some strong results are robust to seed changes but brittle to family and paraphrase shifts.
success_signal: The grid identifies stable effects and brittle effects without requiring a new mechanism.
failure_signal: Replication variance overwhelms claimed mechanism differences.
next_step: Pick three anchor experiments from the compiler program scorecard.

title: Supervision Causality Ablation
question: Is the lift from state-prefix supervision, final-answer supervision, program-token supervision, or data filtering?
hypothesis: State supervision contributes only when state variables map cleanly to task factors.
success_signal: A named supervision source survives shuffled and corrupted controls.
failure_signal: Lift tracks data filtering or token budget rather than the intended representation.
next_step: Start from structured slot and dense supervision ladder anchors.

title: Memory Mode Comparison Suite
question: Which memory use is actually useful: prompt context, constraints, tests, candidates, or failure cases?
hypothesis: Memory is most useful when converted into constraints or tests rather than appended as context.
success_signal: Constraint or test memory beats prompt memory, corrupted retrieval, and no-memory baselines.
failure_signal: All memory modes underperform direct inference or rely on same-family leakage.
next_step: Use independent retrieval and feature-factorized rule experiments as anchors.

title: Constraint Memory Counterexample Generator
question: Can retrieved failures become counterexample generators instead of answer hints?
hypothesis: Failure-case memory can create discriminating tests while reducing direct-answer contamination.
success_signal: Generated counterexamples improve selection under visible-only constraints.
failure_signal: Counterexamples are either redundant with public tests or leak hidden labels.
next_step: Run related-work discovery for counterexample memory and active acquisition.

title: Operator Card Schema Probe
question: What operator-card fields make inventories reusable across experiments?
hypothesis: Examples, invariants, aliases, failure modes, and type signatures are enough for shortlisting and debugging.
success_signal: Cards improve diagnosis or top-k operator retrieval without increasing manual ambiguity.
failure_signal: The schema is too heavy or fails to support held-out operator lookup.
next_step: Sample operator inventory experiments and extract common fields.

title: Operator Bank Shortlister Scaling
question: Can a shortlister preserve selected accuracy as operator banks grow?
hypothesis: A learned top-k shortlister will beat random and heuristic search when trained with held-out primitive families.
success_signal: Top-k cost falls while deployable selected accuracy stays above heuristic baselines.
failure_signal: Coverage rises but selected accuracy drops without a reliable selection plan.
next_step: Use inventory shortlister and scaling-stress experiments as anchors.

title: Active Disambiguation For Type-Colliding Operators
question: Can active probes separate operators that share type signatures but differ semantically?
hypothesis: A few targeted examples can disambiguate type-colliding operators better than passive shortlist scores.
success_signal: Targeted probes improve operator selection per evidence token over random probes.
failure_signal: Disambiguation requires hidden outputs or more budget than full search.
next_step: Identify collisions from current operator inventory runs.

title: Acquisition Policy Comparison Pool
question: Which acquisition rule wins when uncertainty, disagreement, information gain, and learned policies share one pool?
hypothesis: Learned policies help only when trained on features that transfer across task families.
success_signal: A policy improves selection quality per unit evidence across at least two families.
failure_signal: The best policy changes unpredictably across families or loses to fixed-order acquisition.
next_step: Derive pool features from active trace policy and adaptive evidence budget experiments.

title: Family-Aware Evidence Policy
question: Can acquisition policies adapt to date/time, numeric, table, and code task families without overfitting?
hypothesis: Family-aware features improve evidence value, but only when family labels are coarse and deployable.
success_signal: Coarse deployable family features improve budget-normalized selection over no-family controls.
failure_signal: Only oracle family labels help or performance collapses on held-out families.
next_step: Use current active evidence experiments to define feature families.

title: STOP/MORE Controller With Visible Labels
question: Can a controller decide when to gather more evidence using only visible training labels?
hypothesis: A STOP/MORE policy can beat fixed-budget and always-continue heuristics when trained on deployable features.
success_signal: The learned controller improves selected accuracy per cost under matched evidence constraints.
failure_signal: The controller only wins by using hidden labels or by silently spending more budget.
next_step: Anchor on adaptive tool controller and adaptive evidence budget policy experiments.

title: Noisy Tool Controller Stress Test
question: Do process policies still help when tools are flaky, delayed, or misleading?
hypothesis: Robust controllers should learn when to distrust or repeat tool evidence, not just call tools more often.
success_signal: A controller preserves accuracy-cost tradeoffs under noise better than fixed and always-tool baselines.
failure_signal: The policy overuses noisy tools or transfers poorly across noise regimes.
next_step: Use process-control experiments with explicit tool states as anchors.

title: Posttraining Method Shared Substrate
question: How do SFT, DPO, process distillation, and DAgger compare on one candidate/evidence substrate?
hypothesis: Method ranking changes when all methods share data, model, budget, controls, and held-out families.
success_signal: One method shows a robust advantage over frozen, shuffled, and weak-baseline controls.
failure_signal: All gains are explained by data selection, extra tokens, or weak baselines.
next_step: Use DPO, DAgger, and distillation experiments to define shared data.

title: Hard Negative Training Transfer
question: Does training on hard negatives improve real selection, or does it overfit visible failure artifacts?
hypothesis: Hard-negative training helps only when negatives represent held-out families and not just surface artifacts.
success_signal: Mined negatives reduce false positives without lowering recall or transfer.
failure_signal: The model learns benchmark-specific artifacts and fails held-out negative families.
next_step: Start from offline hard-negative and constrained coverage DPO anchors.

title: Adapter-Free Reproducibility Manifest Audit
question: Can every trained-run experiment be audited without committing adapters or checkpoints?
hypothesis: A standardized manifest is enough to preserve provenance while keeping large artifacts external.
success_signal: All trained-run folders either have manifests or explicit no-large-artifact notes.
failure_signal: Important trained outputs cannot be located or reproduced without local private files.
next_step: Use artifact manifest index and readiness matrix to prioritize missing manifests.

title: Diagnostics Failure-Slicing Template
question: Which failure slices should every major experiment report before influencing strategy?
hypothesis: A small standard slice set will change follow-up decisions more often than ad hoc diagnostics.
success_signal: The slices reveal at least one concrete branch, stop, or rerun decision per anchor.
failure_signal: Slices are descriptive but do not change any experiment decision.
next_step: Apply to one compiler, one selector, and one posttraining anchor.

title: Pressure Diagnostic Preflight
question: Can cheap token-pressure and execution-pressure probes predict when an expensive run is worth doing?
hypothesis: Preflight probes can detect capacity or tool-pressure failures before full training or large sweeps.
success_signal: Preflight results correctly predict stop or proceed decisions on multiple mechanisms.
failure_signal: Preflight signals have no relation to full-run outcomes.
next_step: Use token-pressure and tool-control experiments as anchors.

title: Hidden-Label Boundary Linter
question: Can repository checks force every new result to label oracle-only and deployable evidence boundaries?
hypothesis: A lightweight text and metadata linter can prevent hidden-label confusion without slowing pilots.
success_signal: New experiments fail validation when hidden-label boundary documentation is missing.
failure_signal: The rule creates noisy failures or misses obvious oracle/deployable ambiguity.
next_step: Prototype against high-impact selector and oracle-ceiling reports.

title: Reproducibility Scorecards For High-Impact Experiments
question: Which high-impact experiments are reproducible enough to guide future programs?
hypothesis: A scorecard over seeds, data, commands, artifacts, and boundary labels will prioritize curation work.
success_signal: Each high-impact anchor has a reproducibility grade and concrete repair task.
failure_signal: Scorecards duplicate the readiness matrix without adding decision value.
next_step: Use experiment readiness and claim evidence counts to select anchors.

title: Agent Evidence Navigation Test
question: Can a new agent find relevant prior evidence faster using repository indexes than by raw search alone?
hypothesis: Program indexes, claims, readiness, and related-work search reduce duplicate proposals and improve anchor selection.
success_signal: The documented workflow finds better anchors with lower duplicate rate.
failure_signal: Agents ignore or cannot use the generated knowledge surfaces.
next_step: Use existing discovery workflow and related-work CLI.

title: Proposal Deduplication Audit
question: Can the repo detect repeated experiment ideas before they become new runs?
hypothesis: A sample-based audit against catalog, claims, and future queue will catch most near duplicates.
success_signal: The audit catches near duplicates without suppressing genuinely new variants.
failure_signal: Duplicate warnings are too noisy or miss obvious repeated ideas.
next_step: Generate cases from future queue items and recent scaffolded ideas.

title: Multimodal Visual Table Transform Probe
question: Do executable intermediates help small models transform visual table inputs?
hypothesis: Structured intermediate programs will transfer better than direct vision-language answers on held-out visual table layouts.
success_signal: Executable intermediates improve held-out layout accuracy without manual table extraction leakage.
failure_signal: The gain depends entirely on an external OCR or parser oracle.
next_step: Run intake using Foofah and table-transform anchors.

title: UI State Tool Execution Probe
question: Can process-control policies transfer from text/table tools to UI-state observations?
hypothesis: Tool-state schemas can represent UI observations and action history with only small extensions.
success_signal: The controller transfers basic STOP/MORE or repair behavior to UI tasks under a fixed budget.
failure_signal: UI state requires a new representation that invalidates the current process-control schema.
next_step: Prototype with static UI states before live browser control.

title: Synthetic Curriculum Transfer Bakeoff
question: Which synthetic curriculum source transfers best: human-designed, model-generated, or failure-mined?
hypothesis: Failure-mined curricula transfer better than broad model-generated curricula when held-out primitives are explicit.
success_signal: One curriculum source improves held-out performance without hurting direct baselines.
failure_signal: Curriculum gains are IID-only or disappear under matched difficulty.
next_step: Reuse bridge, recombination, and held-out primitive split patterns.

title: Failure-Mined Curriculum Generator
question: Can failure slices automatically generate new training cases that reduce repeated errors?
hypothesis: Failure-mined cases help when tied to a falsifiable slice and held-out control, not when used as unfiltered augmentation.
success_signal: The targeted slice improves without creating new false positives elsewhere.
failure_signal: Generated cases overfit surface patterns or degrade unrelated slices.
next_step: Start with diagnostic failure-slicing template once available.

title: Multi-Model Role Ensemble Pool
question: Can generator, verifier, critic, and evidence-acquirer roles outperform a single small-model policy under fixed total budget?
hypothesis: Role specialization helps only when roles contribute non-redundant evidence and share a strict cost accounting.
success_signal: Role ensembles improve selected accuracy or coverage at matched budget.
failure_signal: All gains come from extra samples or correlated role outputs.
next_step: Use consensus and selector anchors to define the shared pool.

title: Committee Verifier-Critic Loop
question: Can a committee decide when critique or verification is worth another step?
hypothesis: A small committee can learn budget-aware escalation better than fixed critique rounds.
success_signal: Committee control beats fixed rounds and single-controller baselines on accuracy per cost.
failure_signal: Committee discussion increases cost without improving commit quality.
next_step: Start from STOP/MORE controller and consensus experiments.

title: Latency-Ceiling Selector Rerun
question: Which selector or tool-control gains survive strict latency, token, and call ceilings?
hypothesis: Some evidence mechanisms remain useful when converted into early-exit and small-k policies.
success_signal: A constrained policy retains a meaningful fraction of the unconstrained gain over simple baselines.
failure_signal: The method collapses when evidence budget is capped or action latency is counted.
next_step: Pick an existing adaptive controller with a clear smoke path.

title: Compressed Memory Budget Probe
question: Can memory remain useful when context and storage are severely compressed?
hypothesis: Constraint and test memories compress better than raw examples.
success_signal: Compressed constraint or test memory beats no-memory and raw-summary controls.
failure_signal: Compression removes the signal that made memory useful.
next_step: Use memory mode comparison suite once its schema exists.

title: Static Research Navigation Dashboard
question: Can a static dashboard help humans and agents choose better next experiments than Markdown indexes alone?
hypothesis: A dashboard over programs, claims, readiness, artifacts, and future queue will reduce search time and duplicate proposals.
success_signal: Users or agents find higher-quality anchors faster than with raw Markdown navigation.
failure_signal: The dashboard duplicates existing docs without changing decisions.
next_step: Start with generated data files already produced by make catalog.

title: Evidence Branching UI Probe
question: What interface lets a researcher branch from one result into controls, replications, or new programs fastest?
hypothesis: Branch choices improve when claims, readiness gaps, related experiments, and program backlogs are visible in one workflow.
success_signal: Branch proposals cite more relevant evidence and fewer near duplicates.
failure_signal: The interface adds navigation overhead without improving proposal quality.
next_step: Use high-impact claim evidence as the first anchor set.

title: Learned Thinking-Budget Controller
question: Can a deployable controller allocate the thinking-token budget better than the fixed ~1024 optimum, given thinking has an overthinking cost?
hypothesis: A visible-signal STOP/MORE policy over thinking tokens can match or beat a fixed budget at lower mean cost by spending more thinking only on hard inputs.
success_signal: Controller Pareto-dominates fixed budgets on deployable accuracy vs mean thinking tokens, beating a length/difficulty-blind baseline.
failure_signal: No improvement over the best fixed budget once mean token cost is matched.
next_step: Anchor on qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling and the evidence-budget controller experiments.

title: Thinking Content vs Compute (Stronger Control)
question: How much of the native-thinking gain is coherent reasoning versus compute + scaffold + token-presence?
hypothesis: Substituting a different task's thinking (removing token-presence, not just order) will collapse more of the gain than shuffling, isolating the genuine-reasoning share.
success_signal: A clear ordering real > foreign > shuffled > no_think that quantifies the reasoning vs compute/scaffold split.
failure_signal: Foreign-task thinking matches real thinking, i.e. the gain is essentially compute + scaffold, not reasoning.
next_step: Extend qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling controls with foreign-task thinking.

title: Gauntlet Round 3: Does Expert Iteration Compound On The Blackbox Instrument?
question: Re-harvesting the gym with the round-2 model: does the frontier open (glyphgate/loomfix/stallwright), does menagerie keep responding, and does iteration compound or re-saturate (C11 said coverage-bounded; the frontier moved in round 2)?
hypothesis: The round-2 model's collapsed truncation cascade raises harvest yield everywhere, opening the three starved families and buying a second menagerie step; compounding will be smaller than round 2's jump because the emission-seam repair is one-time.
success_signal: Round-3 harvest keepers in glyphgate/loomfix/stallwright at 3x round-1 levels; menagerie quick delta over base grows beyond round 2's +0.22/+0.29 mean on fresh paired seeds.
failure_signal: Yield rises but menagerie plateaus (emission-seam repair was the whole effect) or gym-internal gains stop transferring (locality reappears above the protocol layer).
next_step: Run the fast-profile re-harvest with the round-2 merged model once the fla-kernel medium-tier fault on the host is triaged.
